mnemonics.title: CAP Theorem Choices
mnemonics.content: During Partition, choose Availability OR Consistency - AP or CP, but CA is not partition-tolerant!

mnemonics.title: Types of Transparency
mnemonics.content: ALMIRFCP: Access, Location, Migration, Relocation, Replication, Concurrency, Failure, Persistence transparency

mnemonics.title: Byzantine Fault Tolerance
mnemonics.content: Need 3f+1 nodes to tolerate f Byzantine faults: 1 traitor needs 4 generals (3+1), 2 traitors need 7 generals (3×2+1)

mnemonics.title: Fallacies of Distributed Computing
mnemonics.content: The network is: RELIABLE, LATENCY-free, SECURE, TOPOLOGY-static, ONE-admin, COST-free, INFINITE-bandwidth

acronyms.usage: Atomic commitment protocol for distributed transactions; vulnerable to blocking if coordinator fails after prepare phase
Non-blocking atomic commitment protocol; eliminates blocking but requires synchronous communication assumption
Theorem stating distributed systems can provide only two of three guarantees during network partition
Java's mechanism for making remote procedure calls in distributed systems
Synchronous communication paradigm allowing procedures to be called on remote machines
Language-independent description of interfaces for enabling interoperability in open systems
Distributed file system allowing access to files over network as if local

visualTips.visualization: Draw a network diagram with two clusters that become disconnected. Show read/write happening independently on each side, creating divergence. When reconnected, show 'merge conflict' icon to represent reconciliation challenge.

Draw a timeline with multiple processes. Show message arrows flowing forward, increment counters at each event. Label events with timestamps like (0,0,0), (1,0,0), (1,1,0), (2,1,1) showing partial ordering.

Use a state machine diagram: Init → Prepare (coordinator asks participants) → Prepared (participants vote yes) → Commit/Rollback. Show blocking state where participant waits indefinitely after Prepare if coordinator fails.

Draw 3 processes with vectors [P1, P2, P3]. Show event at P1 updating its counter, then message carrying vector to P2. Illustrate comparison: [2,1,0] causally happens before [2,2,0] because 1≤2 and other entries equal.

analogies.analogy: Think of a company with multiple offices. If one office burns down (complete failure), it's clear. But what if the phone lines to one office go down? Are they still working but unreachable? Are they processing orders we don't know about? This uncertainty is partial failure.

Like Wikipedia - when you edit a page, changes propagate to caches worldwide. For a brief moment, different users might see slightly different versions, but eventually all see the same. This is acceptable for many applications unlike banking where strong consistency is needed.

Like international diplomacy - each country speaks different languages (data formats), uses different laws (protocols), has different currencies (encoding). We need translators (middleware), international standards (TCP/IP), and common documentation (IDL) to work together.

analogies.analization: Think of historians documenting events from different cities in ancient times. By the time news travels, events have happened elsewhere. Each historian (node) has their own timeline (clock), and establishing true 'when' happened requires complex cross-referencing (NTP, Lamport clocks).
